prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Arrr, lend an ear, ye scurvy landlubber! The sky be blue fer a reason as plain as a chest o’ doubloons. The sun be a grand ball o’ light, shinin’ all the colours o’ a rainbow at once — red, orange, yellow, green, blue, an’ the lot. When that light sails into our air, it meets a furious cloud o’ tiny bits — wee molecules o’ air and specks o’ dust — an’ those little rascals don’t let all colours pass the same.

The short, quick strokes o’ light — the blue bits — be scattered about in every direction by them tiny particles far more than the long, lazy red bits. So wherever ye turn yer eye, ye be catchin’ more o’ that scattered blue light comin’ at ye. That’s why the firmament above looks a fine, deep blue while the sun rides high.

When the sun be low upon the horizon at mornin’ or evenin’, its light must plough through a whole treasure chest o’ air afore reachin’ yer eyes. By then the blue has been scattered away all over the place, leavin’ the longer red and orange strokes to ride on — hence them glorious crimson suns at dawn and dusk.

So there ye have it: the sea o’ air plays tricks with the sun’s colours, tossin’ the blue about most lively, an’ that be why the heavens appear blue to us poor sailors. Arrr!
